name: Quote Density Per Sub-Finding
description: Decision and evaluation rubric for how many quotes a qualitative or mixed-methods paper should deploy in support of a single analytical sub-finding. Induced from 9 exemplar papers (Beane 2023 ASQ; Bailey et al. 2012 OS; Barley 1990 ASQ; Beane & Orlikowski 2015 OS; Bernstein 2012 ASQ; Brayne 2017 ASR; Karunakaran et al. 2022 OS; Leonardi 2011 OS; O'Mahony & Bechky 2006 AMJ). Applies to findings sections only; does not govern introduction, theory, methods, or discussion.

scope.applies_to: Qualitative and mixed-methods papers reporting interview, observation, or field-note evidence
scope.does_not_apply_to: Quantitative-forward papers; sections other than Findings; literature review prose

empirical_baseline.source: 9 exemplar papers across ASQ, OS, ASR, AMJ

journal_calibration.ASQ: median 2–3 quotes per sub-finding; heavy use of tables for typologies; field notes deployed alongside quotes
journal_calibration.Organization Science: median 3 quotes per sub-finding; multi-voice corroboration prized for theoretical novelty
journal_calibration.ASR: median 2–3 quotes per sub-finding; variation-documentation framing common
journal_calibration.AMJ: median 1–2 quotes per sub-finding; single strong exemplars often work harder

decision_framework.purpose: Recommend a quote count for a given sub-finding during drafting, or evaluate the count in a draft.

step_1_classify_sub_finding.instructions: Classify the sub-finding into one or more of the types below. Types are not mutually exclusive. If multiple types apply, use the highest default count among them.

types.label: Vivid single instance
types.description: Straightforward illustration of an established or intuitive pattern. Also covers voice/texture work where the speaker's perspective is itself the point.
types.example_from_corpus: O'Mahony & Bechky 2006: camera operator's 'hitting his mark' as illustration of competence differentiation. One quote suffices because the pattern is established and the quote is vivid.

types.label: Mechanism specification
types.description: Claim about HOW something works, not just THAT it happens. Often requires showing both instance and explanation.
types.example_from_corpus: Bernstein 2012: operator on management 'no idea how people operate down here' + nine-year veteran on workers finding better methods but unable to surface them. Two quotes show both the deviance and the barriers to surfacing it.

types.label: Multi-voice generality
types.description: Claim that a pattern holds across roles, levels, or sites. Requires showing the pattern from at least two distinct speaker positions.
types.example_from_corpus: Beane 2023: circulator on team continuity + surgeon on rising experience concentration. Two roles, same pattern.

types.label: Contrast or boundary case
types.description: Sub-finding works by juxtaposition: showing what's different about an opposed case or context.
types.example_from_corpus: Brayne 2017: officer on experiential knowledge ('I already know where the crime's at') + captain challenging that expertise. Contrast shows conflicting logics in the same organization.

types.label: Sequenced or layered mechanism
types.description: Mechanism unfolds in stages: trigger → response → outcome, or one condition enables another. Each stage may need its own anchor quote.
types.example_from_corpus: Bailey et al. 2012: offshore engineer creating symmetric fuel system + Suresh/Vijay debate on penetrations + field notes on guessing. Three quotes show how low access to physical referents cascaded into cultural failure.

types.label: Typology or sub-pattern enumeration
types.description: Sub-finding introduces a typology or set of distinct sub-patterns. Each category needs at least one anchor instance.
types.format_recommendation: If 4+ categories, convert to table rather than narrative quote-stack.
types.example_from_corpus: Barley 1990: four interpretive-discussion patterns (telling, teaching, mutual discussion, reverse teaching) — one example per type. Beane 2023: five workaround types in table format.

types.label: Counterintuitive or novel claim
types.description: Claim challenges prior accounts or established expectations. Extra evidence builds credibility.
types.example_from_corpus: Leonardi 2011: the claim that analysts in different departments framed the same problem differently — anchored with four quotes from four departments (Gene, Jensen Lu, Jason Chan, Kaleeb). Counterintuitive claim builds credibility through diversity of voices.

types.label: Theoretical complement
types.description: Sub-finding establishes a second version of the same mechanism (e.g., strategic AND routine forms; deliberate AND emergent variants). Each variant gets its own anchor, with the complementarity explicitly named in surrounding prose.
types.example_from_corpus: Test case (Hedging with Talent, 4.2): Area Supervisor's strategic 'half the crew won't come in tomorrow' quote paired with the same supervisor's routine 'until I don't see the actual process taking in place I don't attack it.' Two quotes establish that the mechanism operates through both strategic and routine registers — neither alone makes the theoretical point.

types.label: Definitional anchoring
types.description: Sub-finding introduces a new concept the paper will build on. Multiple instances anchor the concept across cases.
types.example_from_corpus: Karunakaran et al. 2022: four response types (justification, apology, stock response, private follow-up) — each anchored with an exemplar.

step_2_escalators.instructions: Starting from the default count, ADD one quote for each condition that applies.

conditions.trigger: A reasonable reviewer would call the claim 'ungrounded' or 'single-source' with the current count
The sub-finding spans multiple speaker roles (manager + worker; junior + senior; multiple sites) and the current quote set hasn't shown that diversity
The sub-finding's theoretical work requires showing BOTH that X happens AND how it happens, and the current quotes only show one
The sub-finding is the paper's named contribution or core concept, and the conceptual handle benefits from instance variety

step_3_de_escalators.instructions: Starting from the post-escalation count, SUBTRACT or CONVERT for each condition that applies.

conditions.trigger: The strongest quote in the set encodes multiple concepts at once and does double duty
A second quote restates the first in different prose without adding new evidence
A quantitative figure or table can carry the variation more efficiently
Quote count reaches 4+ AND the structure is a typology with distinct categories
conditions.action: convert to table rather than narrative quote-stack

step_4_final_check.instructions: For each retained quote, ask: 'What does this quote do that the others don't?' If you cannot name the analytical function in a sentence, cut the quote.

criteria.name: Type Classification Accuracy
criteria.description: For each sub-finding in the findings section, does the deployed quote count match the analytical type of the claim? Counterintuitive claims should not rest on one quote; vivid single instances should not be over-corroborated.

levels.5: Every sub-finding's quote count is well-matched to its type. Vivid single instances stay lean; counterintuitive claims are well-supported; typologies are fully enumerated.
levels.4: Most sub-findings well-calibrated. One or two minor mismatches.
levels.3: Mixed calibration. Some sub-findings under-supported, others over-quoted.
levels.2: Frequent miscalibration. Counterintuitive claims under-evidenced or routine illustrations over-quoted.
levels.1: Systematic miscalibration. Quote counts appear arbitrary.

examples.good: Hedging with Talent 4.2: managerial rationale (type B + G, novel) uses one strong quote; theoretical complement (type H) uses one quote per variant with complementarity named.
examples.bad: Single quote supporting a generalizing claim like 'managers across facilities...' OR four quotes saying the same thing different ways for an intuitive observation.

criteria.name: Quote Function Differentiation
criteria.description: For sub-findings with multiple quotes, does each quote do distinct analytical work? Reader should be able to name what each quote contributes.

levels.5: Each quote serves a nameable function (illustration, mechanism, multi-voice, contrast, etc.) — no quote is redundant.
levels.4: Mostly differentiated. One or two quotes whose distinct work is fuzzy.
levels.3: Some redundancy. Several quotes serve overlapping functions.
levels.2: Substantial redundancy. Multiple quotes paraphrase each other.
levels.1: Quote stacks throughout — quotes pile up without distinct work.

examples.good: Leonardi 2011 on departmental framings: four quotes, each from a different department, each showing a distinct framing of the same problem.
examples.bad: Three quotes all illustrating 'workers were uninformed' in slightly different language.

criteria.name: Escalation Discipline
criteria.description: Does the paper escalate quote count when the claim is novel, counterintuitive, multi-voice, or layered — and avoid escalating for routine illustration?

levels.5: Escalation tracks claim type. Novel claims get 3+; routine illustrations stay at 1.
levels.4: Mostly disciplined. A few escalations missing or overdone.
levels.3: Inconsistent. Some novel claims under-escalated; some routine claims over-escalated.
levels.2: Systematic mis-escalation. Routine claims get heavy quote support; novel claims get one quote.
levels.1: Escalation appears unrelated to claim type.

examples.good: Hedging with Talent 4.2: counterintuitive 'manager-side rationale for not saying the word robot' supported by a single vivid quote because the quote itself encodes the calculation; counter-example claim (R10's 'Carl Lewis' framing) gets one quote because the contrast is the work.
examples.bad: Three quotes deployed to support 'workers do not always know about automation' (an intuitive claim) while a counterintuitive subsidiary claim about cascading shielding gets one fragment.

criteria.name: Anti-Pattern Avoidance
criteria.description: Does the paper avoid quote stacking, restatement, decorative quotes, lone-source over-claiming, and buried typologies?

levels.5: No anti-patterns evident. Every quote earns its place.
levels.4: One or two minor instances. Mostly clean.
levels.3: Several anti-pattern instances. Tightening would improve the paper.
levels.2: Frequent anti-patterns. Findings section reads as quote-heavy.
levels.1: Findings dominated by anti-pattern usage. Quotes feel obligatory rather than analytical.

examples.good: Each quote serves a distinct, nameable analytical function.
examples.bad: Three back-to-back quotes saying the same thing; a quote with no analytical claim attached; a generalizing claim ('managers across the firm...') supported by one quote.

criteria.name: Format Choice
criteria.description: When the sub-finding is a typology or enumeration of variants, does the paper use the right format — narrative quote sequence vs. table?

levels.5: Typologies with 4+ categories use tables; narrative quote sequences are used for 2–3 instances and for variation that needs prose context.
levels.4: Mostly appropriate. One or two cases where a table would have served better, or vice versa.
levels.3: Mixed. Some typologies buried in narrative; some narrative claims forced into table format.
levels.1: Tables and narrative quote sequences appear interchangeable; format choice is not analytical.

examples.good: Beane 2023 workaround typology in table; Hedging with Talent 4.2 worker-opacity quotes in narrative because the variation crosses organizational levels and benefits from prose linking.
examples.bad: Five workaround types each presented as a block quote with full analytical paragraph, when a table would have shown the typology more efficiently.

anti_patterns: Quote stacking — 3+ quotes that illustrate the same point in the same way without adding contrast, mechanism, or variation
Restatement quotes — second quote paraphrases the first in different prose
Decorative quotes — quote present for atmosphere only, with no analytical claim attached
Burying typology in prose — 4+ quotes embedded in flowing paragraphs that would read better as a table
Lone-source over-claiming — single quote supporting a generalizing claim like 'managers across facilities…'
Quote dump — multiple quotes presented sequentially with thin analytical bridging
Orphaned quotes — quote with no analytical setup or follow-through
Quote ventriloquism — quote inserted that doesn't actually make the analytical claim the surrounding prose attributes to it
Symmetric padding — multiple quotes used to make sub-findings appear equally evidenced when they are not

format_substitutes.principle: Tables, field notes, and quantitative figures can do work that quote stacks cannot — and vice versa. Format choice is analytical.
use_table_when: Sub-finding is a typology with 4+ categories
Each category needs an anchor instance but full analytical development is in surrounding prose
Variation across cases is the substantive point
use_field_notes_when: Practice being documented is one workers wouldn't articulate in interviews (workarounds, embodied routines)
Researcher observation captures something the speaker would not name
use_quantitative_figure_when: Variation has a magnitude readers should see directly
Comparison across many cases would require many quotes to convey
use_narrative_quote_sequence_when: Variation crosses organizational levels and prose linking is doing analytical work
Quote does layered work — speaker's framing matters beyond the propositional content
2–3 quotes can carry the variation without becoming a stack

notes.key_principle: Each quote in a findings section should do nameable analytical work distinct from every other quote supporting the same sub-finding. Quote count is determined by the analytical structure of the claim, not by uniform application of a rule.
notes.drafting_vs_evaluation: The decision_framework supports drafting (recommend a count for a given sub-finding). The criteria support evaluation (score a draft's overall quote-density calibration). Both reference the same type taxonomy.
exemplar_sources: Beane 2023 ASQ — Resourcing a Technological Portfolio
Bailey et al. 2012 OS — Lure of the Virtual
Beane & Orlikowski 2015 OS — What Difference Does a Robot Make
Karunakaran et al. 2022 OS — Crowd-based Accountability
notes.first_test_implementation: Hedging with Talent (Beane & Sholler 2026, ASQ submission). Section 4.2 (Information Shielding) was rebuilt during May 2026 revisions using this rubric. The non-strategic shielding insert (type H — theoretical complement) is the clearest live application: one quote per variant (strategic + routine) with the complementarity explicitly named in surrounding prose.
